title: NOC 95103 – Labourers in wood, pulp and paper processing | GTR Immigration
description: As Canada moves toward a sustainable "Bio-economy" in 2026, Labourers in Wood, Pulp and Paper Processing (NOC 95103) are the vital force maintaining the supply chains for eco-friendly packaging and construction materials. Working in sawmills, plywood plants, and pulp mills, these workers are essential to Canada’s $25 billion forest products sector. For international workers, this NOC offers a high-success entry point into the Canadian workforce, particularly in rural and northern communities where these roles are frequently fast-tracked for Permanent Residency.

classification.teer: FeatureDetailsBroad Occupational Category9 – Occupations in manufacturing and utilitiesTEER Category5 (Occupations usually requiring short work demonstration)Major Group95 – Labourers in processing, manufacturing and utilitiesSub-Major Group951 – Labourers in processing and manufacturing

duties: Material Handling: Sorting, piling, and transporting wood logs, lumber, and wood chips manually or using automated conveyor systems.
Operational Support: Assisting machine operators to set up and monitor saws, planers, and pulping equipment.
Cleaning & Safety: Removing wood shavings, scrap lumber, and debris from work areas to ensure a safe industrial environment.
Packaging: Feeding and unloading kilns or specialized drying machines and bundling finished wood products for export.
2026 Update: Utilizing digital handheld scanners to track timber batches and ensure compliance with "Sustainable Forest" certification logs.
employment_requirements: Education: Some secondary school education is usually required. No formal degree is necessary for entry-level roles.
Training: Short-term on-the-job training is provided.
Physical Stamina: Ability to handle heavy materials (up to 25kg) and work in varied environmental conditions (outdoor/indoor).
Safety: Mandatory 2026 WHMIS and site-specific "Safe Work in Forestry" orientation.
salary_prospects: Note: In British Columbia, median wages are significantly higher due to strong union representation in the forestry sector.
job_outlook: The Canadian Occupational Projection System (COPS) indicates that while technology is increasing mill efficiency, the "Human Element" in sorting and safety remains irreplaceable. Over 8,000 job openings are projected due to a high volume of retirements, making this a stable career choice through 2033.
labour_market_demand: The 2026 outlook for wood and paper labourers is driven by the global shift away from plastics toward wood-fiber alternatives:
British Columbia (Interior & North): Very High demand. BC remains the heart of Canada’s forestry sector, with a severe shortage of entry-level labourers in the Prince George and Cariboo regions.
Quebec: Strong prospects. High demand in the Saguenay–Lac-Saint-Jean and Abitibi-Témiscamingue regions for pulp and paper processing support.
New Brunswick: Good prospects. The forestry sector is a primary employer in the province, with consistent needs for mill helpers.
Ontario: Stable demand. Focused in Northern Ontario hubs like Thunder Bay and Sault Ste. Marie.
Why Work in Canada as a Wood/Pulp Labourer?
Sustainable Sector Growth: Be part of an industry leading the way in renewable materials and carbon-sequestering wood products.
Rural Lifestyle & Lower Cost of Living: Most roles are located in beautiful, nature-rich communities where housing is significantly more affordable than in major cities.
Rapid Career Ladder: 12–18 months of experience often leads to promotion into NOC 92014 (Supervisors) or NOC 93102 (Process Operators).
Priority for Regional Pilots: This NOC is a "Gold Standard" for rural immigration pilots, often requiring lower CRS scores for PR.
pathways: Rural and Northern Immigration Pilot (RNIP): A high-success pathway for labourers in wood processing, as many participating communities (like Vernon, BC or Thunder Bay, ON) are forestry hubs.
2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for temporary workers with 12 months of Canadian experience. Forestry labourers are prioritized as "Essential Resource Workers."
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): Provides a direct route for labourers working in the forestry sector in specified regions of British Columbia.
Saskatchewan Hard-to-Fill Skills Pilot: A streamlined pathway for semi-skilled workers with a job offer in the province’s processing sector.
